coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots–aptamer probe and graphene oxide platform for detection of lead (II) ion
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Zhao Sheng
Shan, Xiao Yue
Chai, Lu Jing
Chen, Jian Rong
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: The sensitive detection of heavy metal ions in the organism and aquatic ecosystem using nanosensors based on environment friendly and biocompatible materials still remains a challenge. A fluorescent turn-on nanosensor for lead (II) detection based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide by employment of Pb2+-induced G-quadruplex formation was reported. Graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield, good biocompatibility were prepared and served as the fluorophore of Pb2+ probe. Fluorescence turn-off of graphene quantum dots is easily achieved through efficient photoinduced electron transfer between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide, and subsequent fluorescence turn-on process is due to the formation of G-quadraplex aptamer–Pb2+ complex triggered by the addition of Pb2+. This nanosensor can distinguish Pb2+ ion from other ions with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. The detection method based on this nanosensor possesses a fast response time of one minute, a broad linear span of up to 400.0nM and ultralow detection limit of 0.6nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanosensor
Graphene oxide
Pb2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs)
Principle of graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide based nanosensor for Pb2+ detection
Design and construction of graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide based nanosensor for Pb2+ detection
Detection of Pb2+ based on nanosensor constructed with graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence spectra (A) and time-resolved decay curves (B) of rGQDs, aptamer–rGQDs and aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex. Inset: fluorescence image of aptamer–rGQDs probe.

The quenching effect of GO on the fluorescence of probe (aptamer–rGQDs), fluorescence change by 200nM Pb2+ (red line) and 400nM Pb2+ in the presence of GO (green line) (A), and TEM image of mixture of aptamer–rGQDs probe adsorbed on graphene oxide (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence quenching of aptamer–rGQDs with various concentrations of GO (0.20, 0.41, 0.61, 1.02, 1.43, 2.04, 2.65, 3.26μgmL−1). (B) Time dependence of the fluorescence quenching with 1.43µgmL−1 of GO, and time dependence of the fluorescence recovery with 725.0nM of Pb2+. (C) The fluorescence recovery of aptamer–rGQDs/GO system after incubation with various concentrations of Pb2+ (0.0, 29.0, 87.0, 145.0, 290.0, 435.0, 580.0, 725.0, 870.0, 1015.0, 1160.0, 1305.0nM). (D) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of Pb2+.

Fluorescence intensity changes (I−I 0/I 0) of the sensor in the presence of Ag+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+ (40.0μM) and Pb2+ (1.0μM) in buffer. Inset: the enlarged area.
Schematic illustration of lead (II) detection strategy of nanosensor based on photoinduced electron transfer between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide.
The fluorescence parameters of rGQDs, aptamer–rGQDs and aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Quantum yield determined relative to quinine sulfate.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots–aptamer probe and graphene oxide platform for detection of lead (II) ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensitive detection of heavy metal ions in the organism and aquatic ecosystem using nanosensors based on environment friendly and biocompatible materials still remains a challenge. A fluorescent turn-on nanosensor for lead (II) detection based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide by employment of Pb2+-induced G-quadruplex formation was reported. Graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield, good biocompatibility were prepared and served as the fluorophore of Pb2+ probe. Fluorescence turn-off of graphene quantum dots is easily achieved through efficient photoinduced electron transfer between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide, and subsequent fluorescence turn-on process is due to the formation of G-quadraplex aptamer–Pb2+ complex triggered by the addition of Pb2+. This nanosensor can distinguish Pb2+ ion from other ions with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. The detection method based on this nanosensor possesses a fast response time of one minute, a broad linear span of up to 400.0nM and ultralow detection limit of 0.6nM.

ce:section.ce:para: The monitoring of toxic heavy metal ions is an increasingly important issue because contamination of heavy metal ions may have a serious impact on the ecosystem and present potential risks to human health (Dreyer et al., 2010; Hu et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2012; Vodyanitskii and Yu, 2013). Among these heavy metal ions, Pb2+ is one of the most toxic environmental pollutants. A small amount of Pb2+ can result in nervous system dysfunction, anemia, reproductive dysfunction and developmental disorders, and the more severe case is that exposure to high concentrations of Pb2+ can lead to death (Rubio et al., 2005; Chen et al., 2009; Huang et al., 2010; H. Li et al., 2010; Li et al., 2010a, 2010b, 2012; Sudhakar et al., 2012). However, the traditional detection methods for Pb2+ such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and atomic absorption/emission spectrometry usually need expensive equipment and complex procedures which limit their practical routine monitoring (Butler et al., 2006). Thus, developing a simple, high selective and sensitive and low-cost sensor for rapid detection of Pb2+ in routine monitoring of environmental and biological samples is of great importance.

Many efforts have been contributed to the development of sensors for the simple detection of Pb2+ via cooperative recognition and signaling, in particular, by the employment of optical materials such as organic dyes and functionalized nanomaterials including inorganic quantum dots, and noble metal nanoparticles to construct fluorescent probes (M. Li et al., 2013; X. Li et al., 2013; Lien et al., 2014). However, currently used fluorophores including organic dyes, inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and noble metal nanoparticles in the proposed probes have their own drawbacks. Poor photostability, easy photobleaching, small Stokes shifts and short lifetimes shown by organic dyes push scientists to use QDs or noble metal nanoparticles as the alternative choice since they can overcome these shortcomings. But their high toxicity, common blinking behavior and relatively high cost still do not enable them as an outstanding material as the fluorophores (Wu et al., 2003; Michalet et al., 2005; Somers et al., 2007; Howarth et al., 2008).

Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted a great deal of attention in recent years due to their wide application in biological labeling and imaging, sensors, photovoltaic devices and catalysis since their discovery (H. Li et al., 2010; Li et al., 2010a, 2010b; Yan et al., 2010; Gupta et al., 2011). With respect to conventional organic dyes, semiconductor QDs and noble metal nanoparticles, GQDs possess many superiorities including low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, good resistance to photobleaching, stable emission and easy modulation (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009). As a novel fluorescent materials with good biocompatibility, GQDs have been used for bioimaging and biodetection due to its excellent fluorescence performance (Lin et al., 2011; H.X. Zhao et al., 2011; J. Zhao et al., 2011; Shi et al., 2012). By employment of heteroatom-doped or functionalized GQDs, our group has constructed several chemosensors for metal ions, hydrogen peroxide, glucose and melamine, and biosensors targeting DNA (Zhou et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2014a, 2014b, 2014c; Shan et al., 2014). These sensors involving GQDs probes have an excellent application prospect in detection in vivo and in vitro and are expected to construct implantable nansensors toward continuous physiologic monitoring. Therefore, GQDs were chosen as the outstanding choice as the fluorophore in probe instead of organic dyes, noble metal nanoparticles and semiconductor QDs for construction of Pb2+ sensor in this work.

Graphene oxide (GO) has caused much attention from various fields including molecular sensors, drug delivery, photovoltaic cells, nanocomposites, nanoelectronic devices (Dikin et al., 2007; Song et al., 2010; Zhu et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2013). Graphene oxide as an interesting nanomaterial for sensing, to date, has been employed as an efficient fluorescence quencher based on the photoinduced electron transfer mechanism or energy transfer mechanism (Dong et al., 2010; Loh et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2011). It is found that graphene oxide can be used as a quencher to quench the fluorescent of organic dye, quantum dots and metal nanoparticles. The huge conjugate structure of GO makes it as excellent electron or energy acceptor during possible electron transfer or energy transfer processes, and thus GO shows strong quenching effect on fluorescent probes. The strong π–π interaction between GQDs and GO can easily integrate them as an assembly because they have the similar conjugated structures, which enables them as excellent donor–acceptor pair. Moreover, as a carbon-based material, GO also showed good biocompatibility and thus provides the opportunity of GO-based sensor in the potential application in vivo. Up to now, there are still few work to construct fluorescence detection platform completely use carbon materials with good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Therefore, it has great potential application to build a fluorescent nanosensor in cellular detection by combining excellent fluorescent performance of carbon quantum dots with good platform and quenching properties of graphene oxide.

In this article, a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent nanosensor for Pb2+ detection based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide was constructed. Good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence of GQDs, the formation of G-quadruplex/Pb2+ complex and unique electron transfer between GQDs and GO were taken advantage of to achieve quantitative analysis of Pb2+. The biocompatible GQDs possess high fluorescence quantum yield up to 0.10 after reduction with NaBH4, serving as the fluorophore of highly sensitive probe. GO was prepared and performed as an effective quenching agent. Specific fluorescence “on–off–on” process including fluorescence quenching owing to electron transfer between GQDs and GO, and subsequent fluorescence recovery originated from G-quadruplex/Pb2+ complex due to liberation of free probe from GO surface was employed for the first time to achieve quantitative analysis of Pb2+.

Triplex distilled water was used in the whole experimental process and to prepare PBS buffers. Graphite power, sodium borohydride, tetrahydrofuran, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyllaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), AgNO3, FeSO4·7H2O, Ca(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Hg(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe2(SO4)3 and Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All spectra detections were carried out at pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solution (PBS), which was prepared by mixing stock solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification. DNA oligonucleotide with a concentration of 100.0μM was purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China) and purified using high-performance liquid chromatography, its sequences is listed as follows:
ssDNA: 5′- NH2-(CH2)6-GGGTGGGTGGGTGGGT-3′

GO was synthesized from graphite powder based on a modified Hummer's method according to the literature (Wen et al., 2010; Jiang et al., 2011). Briefly, graphite powder (1.0g) was added to 33mL of cold sulfuric acid (0°C), where KMnO4 (6.0g) was slowly added under continuous stirring in ice-bath. After 15mins, NaNO3 (1.0g) was added into the mixture. The solution was further stirred for 1.5h at 35°C and then an amount of distilled water (40mL) was added. Then the temperature was increased to 95°C and the reaction was allowed for 35min. In the end, the reaction was stopped with the addition of a mixture of 100mL of distilled water and 6mL of H2O2 (30%). The obtained golden dispersion was finally subjected to centrifugation at 1000r.p.m. for 5min to unexfoliated GO. The resulting supernatant solution was washed by distilled water and then subjected to centrifugation at 4000r.p.m until the solution is neutral.

Graphite power (1.0g) was added into a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (180mL) and HNO3 (60mL). The solution was sonicated for two hours and heated under reflux at 80°C for 24h. The mixture was cooled and diluted with deionized (DI) water (800mL). The dark-brown GQDs solution was neutralized with Na2CO3. The final product solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 3 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Chemically reduced GQDs were prepared according to our previous paper (Qian et al., 2014d). The procedure was described briefly as follows: 1.6g of GQDs and excessive sodium borohydride were added into 20mL of tetrahydrofuran solution. Then the mixture was stirred gently for 8h at 70°C. The resulting mixture was subsequently vacuum-distilled to remove excessive sodium borohydride and then washed with ethanol several times, and finally the product solution was dialyzed as mentioned above.

The obtained rGQDs were firstly dissolved in 15.0mL of phosphate buffer (10.0mM, pH 7.4). Then pH of the rGQDs solution was adjusted to 5 to protonate carboxyl groups of rGQDs. After that, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimenthylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) (50.0mg) and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS) (50.0mg) were added to the above solution to activate the surface carboxylic group for 30min at room temperature with continuous stirring. Subsequently, the activated rGQDs and aptamer (20.0μL, 100.0μM) were mixed together and allowed to react for 24h at room temperature with continuous stirring. The aptamer–rGQDs bioconjugates were obtained by centrifugation and washing with PBS buffer three times to remove the free nonconjugated complex.

The quenching effect of GO on fluorescence of aptamer–rGQDs was conducted as follows: 700.0μL of aptamer–rGQDs suspension in buffer solution (PBS buffer, pH 7.4) was prepared at first; then, a series of GO solutions with different concentrations were added into the resultant aptamer–rGQDs solution which were finally diluted to 3.0mL with buffer solution (PBS buffer, pH 7.4); the fluorescence intensities of resulting mixtures were determined using fluorescence spectrometer after 2min of incubation. The detection of Pb2+ was carried out as follows: 700.0μL of aptamer–rGQDs suspension in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) and 3.5µL of GO (1.22mgmL−1) were introduced into the resultant aptamer–rGQDs solution at room temperature to quench the fluorescence. After 2min mixing, different concentrations of Pb2+ were added into the above aptamer–rGQDs solution, and the resulted solutions were finally diluted to 3.0mL. After 1min of incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was measured under the optimal excitation wavelength. The fluorescence dependence on time of quenching and recovery were conducted by monitoring fluorescence intensity at different incubation times.

The morphologies of the products were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples were prepared by dropping ethanolic or aqueous suspensions of the separated fractions of oxidized products onto Cu TEM grids coated with a holey amorphous carbon film and following solvent evaporation in a dust protected atmosphere. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 165nm hemispherical electron energy analyzer. The incident radiation came from monochromatic Al X-ray (1486.6eV) at 15kV and 3mA. Wide survey scans were taken at an analyzer pass energy of 160eV over a 1400–0eV binding energy with 1.0eV step and a dwell time of 100ms, while narrow multiplex higher resolution scans were performed at a pass energy of 20eV with 0.05eV step and a dwell time of 200ms. The pressure in analysis chamber was less than 7.5×10−9 Torr during sample analysis. The UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer, in which the products were dispersed in solvent after ultrasonication for 30min. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer, and lifetimes were determined using a FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Determination of the quantum yields of fluorescent substances was accomplished by comparison of the wavelength integrated intensity of these functionalized products to that of the standard quinine sulfate. The optical density was kept below 0.05 to avoid inner filter effects. The quantum yields of fluorescent substances were calculated using
Ф =
Ф
s
[
( I
A
s
n
2
)
/
(
I s
A
n s
2
)
]

where Ф is the quantum yield, I is the integrated intensity, A is the optical density and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript S refers to the standard reference of known quantum yield. Quinine sulfate was chosen as the standard, whose quantum yield is 0.577 and nearly constant for excitation wavelength from 200nm to 400nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide based nanosensor for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots have shown good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence and easy manipulation, and thus were chosen as the fluorophore in the lead (II) probe. Graphene oxide with unique two-dimensional structure and good dispersion in water after moderate oxidation were used as electron acceptor and quenching agent. The aptamer with the sequence of 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-GGGTGGGTGGGTGGGT-3′ was reported to specifically bind to Pb2+ (H. Li et al., 2010; Li et al., 2010a, 2010b), and thus used as the functional group targeting at lead (II) in the probe. Scheme 1 displays the schematic illustration of lead (II) detection strategy based on photoinduced electron transfer between rGQDs labeled probe and GO. To improve the fluorescence of probe and sensitivity of the as-prepared nanosensor, GQDs were reduced with NaBH4 to prepare rGQDs with high emission efficiency. Subsequent detection of target Pb2+ is accomplished by three following steps. In the first step, aptamer probe (aptamer–rGQDs) is synthesized using the aptamer and rGQDs through condensation reaction with EDC. The as-prepared aptamer–rGQDs probe possesses strong green fluorescence owing to the high quantum yield of rGQDs. In the second step, graphene oxide (GO) with good solubility in water was prepared through oxidation by concentrated nitric and sulfuric acid firstly, and then aptamer–rGQDs probe is mixed with GO so that aptamer–rGQDs can assemble on the surface of GO through electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction, where the assembly aptamer–rGQDs/GO forms. The formation of assembly aptamer–rGQDs/GO leads to nearly complete fluorescence quenching of aptamer–rGQDs probe through photoinduced electron transfer between rGQDs and GO (Yu et al., 2014). In the third step, upon the addition of Pb2+, Pb2+ can strongly coordinate with the aptamer in the probe to form the G-quadruplex/Pb2+ complex. The formation of G-quadruplex/Pb2+ complex breaks up electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction between aptamer–rGQDs and GO, and thus results in good separation between G-quadruplex/Pb2+ complex and GO in solution. Therefore, the fluorescence from rGQDs in the G-quadruplex/Pb2+ complex turns on again, and gradually increases upon the continuous addition of Pb2+ into the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and construction of graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide based nanosensor for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation and characterization of rGQDs were described in detail in our pervious paper (Qian et al., 2014d). It has been proven that the reduction of GQDs with NaBH4 can substantially improve the emission efficiency of carbon nanodots (Zheng et al., 2011). Thus, pristine GQDs obtained by the oxidation of graphite powder were reduced by sodium borohydride to obtain reduced GQDs (rGQDs) with strong fluorescence. GO was synthesized from graphite powder based on a modified Hummer’s method (Wen et al., 2010; Jiang et al., 2011). As-prepared GO was readily dispersed in water due to the introduction of a great deal of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups at the surface. Good dispersion offers the key factor for GO to act as a good quenching agent. The aptamer as the complex ligand was modified with amino group for the subsequent link with rGQDs. The condensation reaction was conducted between –COOH group of rGQDs and –NH2 group of the aptamer with EDC reagent under the catalysis of Sulfo-NHS.

Fig. 1 shows the fluorescence spectra and time-resolved decay curves of all fluorescent species involved in the detection route. After reduction with NaBH4, rGQDs exhibit strong green light with central emission band at 456nm, and their fluorescence quantum yield is improved to 0.10, which enables the strong fluorescence of the following fluorescent probe. The emission peak of aptamer–rGQDs probe is blue-shifted to 435nm relative to that of rGQDs at the optimal excitation wavelength of 300nm (Figs. S1 and S2 in SI). The large shift in emission peak between rGQDs and aptamer–rGQDs indicates the successful synthesis of aptamer–rGQDs. Their distinct time-resolved fluorescence decay curves and substantial difference in lifetime provide another piece of evidence for chemically bonded link between aptamer and rGQDs. Furthermore, their different UV–vis spectra shown in Fig. S3 further confirmed the formation of aptamer–rGQDs. The as-prepared probe in water exhibits strong green fluorescence under the UV lamp as shown in Fig. 1 inset. In order to explore the fluorescent characteristics of aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex, a certain amount of Pb2+ was added into the aptamer–rGQDs solution to form aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex. Upon the addition of Pb2+, the central emission peak of aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ is not shifted relative to that of aptamer–rGQDs, but their emission band shapes have apparent difference, implying the formation of aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex. The difference between aptamer–rGQDs and aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex in time-resolved fluorescence decay curve and lifetime further confirms the formation of aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex upon the introduction of Pb2+ into aptamer–rGQDs probe.

To assess the practicality of detection strategy proposed in Scheme 1, we carried out the following test experiments as shown in Fig. 2A. When a certain amount of GO (2.0µgmL−1) was added into the solution of aptamer–rGQDs probe, the fluorescence intensity was sharply decreased to a half of the original value, i.e., the aptamer–rGQDs probe adsorbed on the surface of GO thus leading to the strong quenching of the fluorescence. After a certain amount of Pb2+ was added into the above solution, the fluorescence was largely recovered to three quarter of the original intensity. This suggested that the added Pb2+ coordinated with the aptamer in the probe to form aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex, and the formation of the complex resulted in the separation between GO and aptamer–rGQDs hence turning off the electron transfer between rGQDs and GO. The recovered fluorescence can be attributed to the free aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex. To reveal the strong quenching effect of GO in this on–off–on process, fluorescence change of the aptamer–rGQDs in the presence single Pb2+ was also performed for comparison with that influenced by combined GO/Pb2+. When only an amount of Pb2+ was added into the aptamer–rGQDs probe solution, only slight enhancement of fluorescence can be observed. This weak fluorescence enhancement can be attributed to relatively high quantum yield of aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex with respect to that of aptamer–rGQDs. This can be confirmed by their respective quantum yield listed in Table 1. In order to verify the attachment of aptamer–rGQDs on the surface of GO in the quenching process, we characterized the mixture by TEM as shown in Fig. 2B. Fig. 2B clearly illustrates that a great deal of aptamer–rGQDs were adsorbed on the surface of GO. It is also shown that the size distribution of 3–5nm in diameter and characteristic crystalline structure of GQDs, consistent with the previous results (Qian et al., 2014b). This experiment offered solid evidence for strong quenching of aptamer–rGQDs probe from GO through close interaction between them. The apparent comparison in amounts of observed aptamer–rGQDs on the surface of graphene oxide shown in Fig. S4 clearly illustrated the formation of aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ and their liberation from the surface of graphene oxide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ based on nanosensor constructed with graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the analytical performance for quantitative analysis of Pb2+, we firstly optimized the detection conditions including incubation time for fluorescence quenching by GO and subsequent fluorescence enhancement by Pb2+, and the optimal amount of added GO for fluorescence quenching. Fig. 3A shows the quenching effect with various concentrations of GO, and the fluorescence in intensity gradually decreases with increasing the concentration of GO. When the concentration of GO is up to 3.3µgmL−1, the fluorescence intensity remains nearly unchanged. It is found that aptamer–rGQDs/GO system including a large amount of GO up to 3.3µgmL−1 showed non-stable fluorescence intensity and cannot be readily enhanced by the addition of Pb2+ due to the presence of too much GO in the system. Thus, 1.43µgmL−1 was chosen as the optimal concentration of GO. The optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by GO and fluorescence recovery by Pb2+ is displayed in Fig. 3B. One can notice that the fluorescence intensity quenched by GO attains stable within only 2min, while the fluorescence intensity enhanced by added Pb2+ remains nearly identical after 1min incubation, suggesting that the response time is very fast with respect with those in the previous reports on detection of Pb2+ (M. Li et al., 2013; X. Li et al., 2013). Hence 2min and 1min were used as the incubation time for fluorescence quenching and fluorescence enhancement in the following determination of Pb2+ respectively.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this nanosensor was evaluated by adding varying concentrations of Pb2+ into the proceeding aptamer–rGQDs/GO solution. The enhanced fluorescence comes from aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complex formed between aptamer–rGQDs and Pb2+ after addition of Pb2+ into aptamer–rGQDs/GO system. As demonstrated in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity is increased gradually with continuous addition of Pb2+ because more and more aptamer–rGQDs/Pb2+ complexes form and escape from GO surface during this process. When the concentration of Pb2+ in the solution is up to 1305.0nM, the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged because of nearly complete consumption of aptamer–rGQDs probe in the sensor by added Pb2+. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Pb2+ shown in Fig. 3D was established, and can be expressed as y=0.262x+181.26, where R 2=0.999. The established method for Pb2+ detection has a broad linear range of 9.9–435.0nM with a detection limit of 0.6nM as estimated from the derived calibration curve (>3 standard deviations). Although the detection limit of this method is slightly inferior to that based on semiconductor quantum dots (0.09nM) (M. Li et al., 2013), and comparable to that based on organic dye as fluorophore (0.4nM) (X. Li et al., 2013), its linear detection range (9.9–435.0nM) is much broader than those based on semiconductor quantum dots (0.1–10nM) and organic dye (2–50nM) (M. Li et al., 2013; X. Li et al., 2013).

Selectivity as another important performance parameter for a practical sensor was also assessed. The fluorescence response of the aptamer–rGQDs/GO ensemble assay was evaluated against common metal ions including Ag+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+ and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4, 1.0μM of Pb2+ leads to quite sharp increase in fluorescence intensity as much as ten times of the blank, whereas no obvious change in fluorescence intensity was observed after the addition of 40.0μM of other metal ions, indicating the high selectivity for Pb2+. It should be noted that the introduction of Hg2+ and Ag+ leads to a slightly reverse response relative to the presence of Pb2+, which is much helpful to distinguish Pb2+ and Hg2+ by this sensor because it is known that it is usually hard to identify the two heavy metal ions separately. This is the first time to achieve sensitive analysis of Pb2+ using nanosensor based on photoinduced electron transfer between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide. It has greater advantage in biocompatibility and economical efficiency than semiconductor quantum dots and noble metal nanoparticles based method for Pb2+ detection.

In summary, a novel and effective carbon materials-based nanosensor through regulating the interaction between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide for detection of Pb2+ has been established. The prepared aptamer–rGQDs probe targeting at Pb2+ has superiorities in strong fluorescence, good biocompatibility and resistance to photobleaching since graphene quantum dots serve as the fluorescent labeling function. Photoinduced electron transfer between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide was employed to achieve the controllable fluorescence turn-on process. This nanosensor can distinguish Pb2+ and other ions with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. It shows a quite broad linear scope and ultralow detection limit for Pb2+ detection, and thus possesses excellent performance in trace analysis. Since all the materials involved in the sensing system are of excellent biocompatibility, it is expected that this Pb2+ detection method could be used in vivo and in vitro as well as to construct implantable nansensors toward continuous physiologic monitoring. We hope these results can promote the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in routine monitoring of environmental and biological field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405142, 21005073 and 21275131) and Zhejiang Province (Nos. LY13B050001, LQ13B050002 and ZJHX201403).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.12.057.
